articles.title: Brickbat: Who's Laughing Now?
articles.content:
俄亥俄州警方逮捕了沃特福德高中教师马修·沃登，指控他两项袭击罪名，据称他在体育课上击打了其中一名学生的下巴，并试图击打另一名学生。根据法庭文件，沃登告诉学生们他们"让他很生气"，并指示他们"伸出下巴"，然后挥拳出击。他击中了一名学生，但另一名学生躲开了，据报导被击中的学生脸上留下红印并感到下巴疼痛。调查人员表示，沃登确认事件确实发生，但声称他只是在和学生们"开玩笑"。学校官员表示，学区仍在进行调查。

文章砖块：现在谁在笑？最初发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
Police in Ohio arrested Waterford High School teacher Matthew Warden on two charges of assault for allegedly punching one student in the jaw and trying to punch another during gym class. According to court documents, Warden told the students they were "pissing him off" and instructed them to "stick out their chins" before throwing punches. He missed one student but made contact with the other, reportedly leaving him with a red mark and jaw pain. Investigators said Warden confirmed that the incident happened but claimed he was "only joking around" with the students. School officials say the district is still investigating.

The post Brickbat: Who's Laughing Now? appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Alexis Wilkins, FBI Director's Kash Patel's Girlfriend, Sues MS Now for Defamation
articles.content:
以下为威尔金斯诉韦尔萨恩特媒体集团案（田纳西州中区联邦地区法院）投诉书内容，于周五提交：

本诽谤诉讼涉及MS Now（原MSNBC）使用虚假的"匿名"消息源，传播明知虚假或漠视真相的指控，声称亚历克西斯·威尔金斯通过与FBI局长卡什·帕特尔的关系，滥用FBI资源。被告当然有权评论FBI的领导层及其资源分配，无论正面或负面。然而，他们无权对此撒谎。

被告虚假声称，威尔金斯女士要求、帕特尔局长命令负责她安保的小组——该小组当时根本不存在——护送一名醉酒的朋友在"一夜狂欢"后回家。他们虚假地将威尔金斯女士描绘为醉酒状态，尽管明知她不饮酒。被告以为可以通过引用"匿名消息源"、虚伪地声称掌握"非公开"和"内部"信息来逃避责任。但这完全是胡扯，他们自己也心知肚明。记者不能通过躲在捏造的"匿名"消息源背后来逃避责任。本诉讼旨在追究被告恶劣谎言的责任……

2025年12月5日，被告MS Now发表了一篇文章，由其员工卡罗尔·利奥尼格和肯·迪拉尼安撰写，标题为《消息人士称：卡什·帕特尔命令FBI安保小组护送女友的朋友回家》。文章中被告写道：

据三位知情人士透露，FBI局长卡什·帕特尔曾多次命令保护其女友的安保小组护送她一名据称醉酒的朋友在纳什维尔一夜狂欢后回家。

消息人士称，帕特尔的女友亚历克西斯·威尔金斯至少两次要求她安保团队中的FBI特工送她朋友回家，其中包括今年春天一次，但特工们反对偏离任务。消息人士表示，但帕特尔坚持让他们按威尔金斯的要求行事，其中一次他还打电话给威尔金斯安保小组的负责人，对他吼叫要求执行。

这完全虚假。帕特尔局长从未命令任何FBI特工或威尔金斯女士安保小组的成员护送威尔金斯女士的任何朋友回家——无论是否醉酒——威尔金斯女士也从未要求他们这样做。这些所谓的请求/命令不仅从未发生，而且整个情节都是捏造的。没有任何FBI特工曾护送威尔金斯女士的任何朋友回家。

被告在文章中声称其诽谤指控的内容据称发生在2025年春天。值得注意的是，威尔金斯女士当时并没有安保小组。被告明知这一点。被告此前已于2025年11月17日发表了一篇关于威尔金斯女士安保小组的完整文章。被告是首发该新闻的媒体，因为威尔金斯女士当时刚刚被分配了安保小组，这是基于针对她的可信死亡威胁而必需的……

2025年12月2日——即本案涉案文章发布前三天——被告迪拉尼安联系了FBI发言人本·威廉姆森，就威尔金斯女士的安保小组被调去护送她朋友的指控征求意见。被告在文章中严重歪曲和贬低了FBI的回应，写道：

FBI发言人本·威廉姆森没有回答关于威尔金斯安保小组被调离执行任务的多个内部说法的问题，但广泛否认此类事件发生过。

"这是编造的，没有发生，"威廉姆森说。

【自首次发布以来，被告未经任何说明地对文章进行了悄悄修改。在当前网络版本中，该句现为："FBI发言人本·威廉姆森广泛反驳了此类事件发生过。"】

这在两方面是不诚实的。首先，威廉姆森先生确实回答了迪拉尼安先生关于这些无根据指控的问题。他不只是"广泛"否认，而是具体地、有针对性地在经过与所有潜在证人沟通调查后，在记录中予以反驳，并解释没有记录或佐证。其次，是迪拉尼安拒绝回答问题。威廉姆森对迪拉尼安完全没有细节或佐证感到震惊，要求他提供任何支持指控的内容。他们的短信往来如下：

威廉姆森：你邮件中提到的这个安保事件看起来是编造的。我刚核实过。没有任何记录，亚历克西斯根本不喝酒，说这不是真的。局长也这么说。你有更多细节吗？大概日期？那个朋友是谁？任何信息都行。

迪拉尼安：请稍等

威廉姆森：你不是已经有这些信息了吗？

迪拉尼安：明确一下，没有人说是亚历克西斯醉酒。我们没有你在找的细节，但我们对消息源很满意。所以只是想要你们的官方回应。

威廉姆森：所以你们没有名字，没有日期范围，什么都没有——只是对消息源很满意。你是认真的吗？

敬上

因此，被告在发布前明确知道FBI已调查了这些指控并予以反驳。被告迪拉尼安不仅漠视这一事实，声称"我们对消息源很满意"，而且被告在文章中隐瞒了这一信息，虚假地暗示FBI只是做出了本能的、笼统的否认，并虚假地声称FBI拒绝回答问题。

此外，被告迪拉尼安在对FBI的短信往来中撒谎，虚假声称他没有关于指控事件大概日期的信息。如果迪拉尼安提供了春天的时间框架，FBI本可以、也将会更加确凿地反驳这一报道，因为可以指出威尔金斯女士当时根本没有安保小组。

被告以精心算计的方式回避了这一事实。他们通过自己在11月17日的文章知道威尔金斯女士最近才被分配安保小组。他们知道，如果他们给FBI提供春天的时间框架，结果将不仅仅是他们寻求的"官方回应"，而且会破坏他们想要的叙事。

由于所谓事件并未发生，威尔金斯女士安保小组的所有潜在证人，包括每一位成员，都断然、正确地否认了文章中的指控。由于FBI没有佐证记录，被告的"匿名"消息源不可能有第一手知情。如果这些消息源确实存在，被告也是明知或漠视其完全缺乏知识和可信度。被告知道这一点，并在文章中遭到记录在案的反驳的情况下，仍冒失地选择发布这些匿名来源的谎言。

事实上，被告在文章中称其消息源"被授予匿名权以讨论非公开事务"，以及他们是"内部说法"，这虚假地向读者暗示——正如其意图所示——这些消息源是官方的，甚至就是威尔金斯女士安保小组的成员。仅此一点就是被告恶意故意混淆和知悉虚假性的证据……

正如被告迪拉尼安含蓄承认的那样，文章还暗示威尔金斯女士当时处于醉酒状态。通过声称她多次在纳什维尔与一群醉酒的朋友"一夜狂欢"后深夜外出——纳什维尔市以深夜狂欢和饮酒著称——文章向普通读者暗示威尔金斯女士是个重度饮酒者。这完全虚假，因为威尔金斯女士几乎不喝酒，如有的话也极少。重要的是，被告从威廉姆森先生的短信中得到了该暗示虚假的真实通知……

诉状还（1）指控相关陈述系以"实际恶意"作出（即明知或漠视虚假），（2）主张"威尔金斯女士不是公众人物"，因此实际恶意标准不适用，她"只需证明被告存在过失"，以及（3）批评了确立实际恶意要求的《纽约时报》诉沙利文案。该最后一点在诉状中当然不是特别相关，但威尔金斯可能计划在最高法院被说服削减该先例，或削减其从公职人员（沙利文案中的精确问题）扩展至公众人物的范围时，提出并保留此类挑战。诉状还包括一项"虚假形象"主张，这与诽谤主张密切相关：它同样基于相关陈述据称虚假，但寻求的救济是因其对原告和理性之人"极为令人反感"，而非因其损害名誉。

《亚历克西斯·威尔金斯——FBI局长卡什·帕特尔的女友——起诉MS Now诽谤》一文首发于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
From the Complaint in Wilkins v. Versant Media Group. Inc. (M.D. Tenn.), filed Friday:

This defamation lawsuit is about MS Now (formerly, MSNBC) using sham "anonymous" sources to push knowingly or recklessly false allegations that Alexis Wilkins, through her relationship with FBI Director Kash Patel, abused FBI resources. Defendants are, of course, free to comment on the leadership of the FBI and its allocation of resources, whether positively or negatively. They are not, however, entitled to lie about it.

Defendants falsely asserted that Ms. Wilkins demanded, and Director Patel ordered, that federal agents assigned to her security detail—which did not even exist at the time—escort an intoxicated friend home after a "night of partying." They falsely portrayed Ms. Wilkins as being intoxicated even knowing that she does not drink. Defendants presumed they could get away with this fiction by citing to "anonymous sources," disingenuously claiming "nonpublic" and "inside" knowledge. This was hogwash and they knew it. Journalists cannot avoid accountability by hiding behind fabricated "anonymous" sources. This lawsuit seeks to bring accountability for Defendants' egregious lies….

On December 5, 2025, Defendant, MS Now, published an article, written by its employees, Defendants Carol Leonnig and Ken Dilanian, titled, "Kash Patel ordered FBI detail to give girlfriend's pal a lift home: sources" …. In the Article, Defendants wrote:

FBI Director Kash Patel has—on more than one occasion—ordered that the security detail protecting his girlfriend escort one of her allegedly inebriated friends home after a night of partying in Nashville, according to three people with knowledge of the incidents.

Patel's girlfriend, Alexis Wilkins, asked FBI agents on her security team at least two times, including once this spring, to drive her friend home, and agents objected to diverting from their assignment, said the sources, who were granted anonymity to discuss nonpublic matters. But Patel insisted they do as Wilkins requested and in one case called the leader of Wilkins' security detail and yelled at him to do so.

This is entirely false. Director Patel has never ordered any FBI agent or member of Ms. Wilkins' security detail to escort any of Ms. Wilkins' friends home—inebriated or otherwise—nor did Ms. Wilkins ask any of them to do so. Not only did these supposed demands/orders never take place, but the entire scenario is fabricated. No FBI agents have ever escorted any of Ms. Wilkins' friends home.

Defendants claimed in the Article that the substance of their defamatory allegations supposedly occurred in Spring 2025. Notably, Ms. Wilkins did not have a security detail at that time. Defendants were aware of this. Defendants had previously published, on November 17, 2025, an entire article about Ms. Wilkins' security detail. Defendants were the first to break that story as Ms. Wilkins had only then been recently assigned a detail, necessary due to credible death threats made against her….

On December 2, 2025—three days prior to publication of the Article at issue in this case—Defendant Dilanian reached out to FBI spokesman, Ben Williamson, to obtain comment on the accusation that Ms. Wilkins' detail had been diverted to escort her friends home. Defendants grossly misrepresented and diminished the FBI's response in the Article, writing:

FBI spokesperson Ben Williamson did not answer questions about multiple inside accounts of Wilkins' detail being diverted, but broadly denied such events took place.

"This is made up and did not happen," Williamson said.

{Since its original publication, Defendants have stealth-edited the Article, without any acknowledgement of the change. In the current online version, the sentence reads: "FBI spokesperson Ben Williamson broadly disputed that such events took place."}

This was dishonest in two respects. First, Mr. Williamson did answer Mr. Dilanian's questions about these unfounded accusations. He did not just "broadly" deny them. He specifically, and pointedly, refuted them on the record after having researched the matter by speaking to all potential witnesses, explaining that there is no record or corroboration. Second, it was Dilanian who refused to answer questions. Williamson, who was flabbergasted at Dilanian's complete lack of details or corroboration, asked him for anything that would support the accusations. Their text exchange was as follows:

WILLIAMSON: This detail thing you emailed about looks like it's made up. I just checked. No record of it anywhere and Alexis, who doesn't even drink, said it's not true. As did Director. Do you have any more details? General date? Who is the friend? Anything.

DILANIAN:  Stand by

WILLIAMSON:  Do you not have this info already?

DILANIAN: Just to be clear no one is saying Alexis was drunk. We don't have the details you are looking for but we are comfortable with our sourcing. So just looking for your official comment.

WILLIAMSON:  So you have no name, no date range, no nothing – just comfortable with your sourcing. Are you serious?

Respectfully

Defendants were, therefore, specifically aware prior to publication that the FBI had investigated the allegations and refuted them. Not only did Defendant Dilanian recklessly disregard this fact, claiming "we are comfortable with our sourcing," but Defendants omitted this information from the Article, falsely implying that the FBI made only a reflexive and broad denial, and falsely claiming that the FBI had refused to answer questions.

Additionally, Defendant Dilanian lied to the FBI in the text exchange, falsely claiming that he had no information on the general date of the alleged incident. Had Dilanian provided the Spring timeframe for the allegation, the FBI could, and would have even more conclusively refuted the story by pointing out that Ms. Wilkins had no security detail at that time.

Defendants, in calculated fashion, avoided that truth. They knew about the recent assignment of Ms. Wilkins' detail by virtue of their own November 17 article. They knew that if they were to give the FBI the Spring timeframe, it would result in more than just the "official comment" they were looking to get and would derail their desired narrative.

Because the alleged events did not take place, all potential witnesses, including every member of Ms. Wilkins' security detail flatly, and rightly, deny the allegations from the Article. And as the FBI has no corroborating records, Defendants' "anonymous" sources could not possibly have had first-hand knowledge. If their sources existed at all, Defendants knowingly or recklessly disregarded their complete lack of knowledge and credibility. Defendants knew this and recklessly chose to publish these anonymously sourced lies in the face of on-the-record refutation.

In fact, by saying in the Article that their sources were "granted anonymity to discuss nonpublic matters," and that they were "inside accounts," Defendants falsely suggest to their readers, as intended, that the sources are official, or even members of Ms. Wilkins' security detail. This alone is evidence of Defendants' maliciously deliberate obfuscation and knowledge of falsity….

As implicitly acknowledged by Defendant Dilanian, the Article also implies that Ms. Wilkins was inebriated. By claiming that on multiple occasions she was out late after a "night of partying" with a group of inebriated friends in Nashville, Tennessee—a city known for late-night partying and drinking—the Article suggests to the average reader that Ms. Wilkins is a heavy drinker. This is entirely false, as Ms. Wilkins very rarely drinks, if ever. Importantly, Defendants were on actual notice that this implication was false from Mr. Williamson's text message….

The complaint also (1) alleges that the statements were said with "actual malice" (i.e., were knowingly or recklessly false, (2) argues that "Ms. Wilkins is not a public figure," so that the actual malice standard doesn't apply, and instead "she need only show that Defendants acted negligently," and (3) criticizes New York Times v. Sullivan, which set forth the actual malice requirement. That last point is of course not particularly relevant in a Complaint, but presumably Wilkins is planning to raise and preserve such a challenge in the event that the Supreme Court can be persuaded to cut back on that precedent, or cut back its extension from public officials (the precise issue in Sullivan) to public figures. The complaint also includes a "false light" claim, which is closely related to a defamation claim: It also rests on the statements being allegedly false, but seeks to recover for their being "highly offensive" to the plaintiff and to a reasonable person, rather than for their damaging reputation.

The post Alexis Wilkins, FBI Director's Kash Patel's Girlfriend, Sues MS Now for Defamation appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: "Ukraine Turns Real-Life Kills into Video Game Thrills for Drone Pilots"
articles.content:
《乌克兰将现实杀戮变成无人机操作员的视频游戏快感》

华盛顿邮报今日刊登了一篇题为《乌克兰将现实杀戮变成视频游戏体验》的文章，以下是其中的几个段落：

攻击型无人机在乌克兰东部的一片田野上空发现了一名俄罗斯士兵，随即俯冲而下。直到它接近目标时，那名士兵才注意到它的存在。机载摄像头实时将画面传回给远程操作员，记录下了他的惊恐反应。他高举双手，开始奔跑。视频画面随即中断。

随后，第二段监控录像出现了：士兵的尸体一动不动地躺在田野上。无人机镜头拉近，显示出他毫无生气的面孔……

这些视频被提交给乌克兰政府，作为前线无人机操作员之间竞赛的参赛作品，得分高者可获得积分和奖励——这在战争日益视频游戏化的背景下，成为了一种真正的第一人称射击游戏。各部队每使一名俄罗斯士兵丧失行动能力或死亡，每摧毁一件武器、车辆或军事装备，都可获得积分。积分可以在政府在线市场上兑换成更多无人机，从而可以针对更多俄罗斯军队。

这是一个有趣的故事，我没有理由怀疑其事实的准确性。但我觉得它缺乏历史背景。我不是军事历史学家，但让我阐述一下我的想法；我很想听听更了解此事的读者有什么看法。

据我了解，使用电影录像来确认敌机击落的常规做法至少可以追溯到第二次世界大战。而且，众所周知，确认击杀数量的竞赛在当时就已存在；军事人员一直像其他人一样重视成功的声誉。

就乌克兰无人机故事而言，如果有什么新意的话，那就是乌克兰军方将监控击落飞机的系统扩展到了针对单个士兵的击杀上。我不确定这是否真的有多新颖：我听说无人机有时被用来确认狙击手的击杀，但我不确定这种做法有多普遍或系统化。

至于奖励，如果实物奖励确实只是更多的无人机，这听起来是一个相当明显的方法：给最有效的部队提供更多的资源。据我了解，苏联陆军中表现特别突出的部队被标记为“近卫”部队，并获得了更好的装备。但我预计在其他时代和地方也有类似做法。

此外，苏联在二战期间曾因（特别是通过照片确认的）击落敌机而给予货币奖励；即使是共产主义者也能欣赏金钱激励的价值。以下是斯大林1942年6月17日命令中的一个条文（另见《伟大的卫国战争中的王牌》以及194 1年8月19日的命令）：

为了激励战斗机飞行员的作战工作，[我命令从今年6月20日起]建立以下金额的货币奖励：

— 每击落一架敌方轰炸机支付2000卢布；
— 每击落一架敌方运输机支付1500卢布；
— 每击落一架敌方战斗机支付1000卢布……

只有在地面部队、照片和多个机组报告确认摧毁敌机的情况下，才支付摧毁敌机的费用。

以战场额外装备作为奖励似乎不那么引人注目。

因此，再次强调，《华盛顿邮报》的这篇文章既有趣又信息丰富，特别是为了分配额外武器而记录单个士兵击杀的无人机录像的特定细节。但我原本期望它能更多地与军事录像记录的历史以及对成功行动的军事奖励联系起来，而不是仅仅将其描述为“史无前例”。我很想听听比我更了解此事的读者对此有何看法。
articles.summary:
That's the title and subtitle of a Washington Post article today; here are a couple of excerpts:

The attack drone spots the Russian soldier in a field in eastern Ukraine and swoops in. Only when it's nearly upon him does he see it. The onboard camera, sending video back to the remote pilot in real time, captures his panic. He throws his hands above his head and begins to run. The video cuts out.

Then a second video, shot from a surveillance drone: The soldier's body lies in the field, motionless. The drone zooms in to show his apparently lifeless face….

[T]hese videos … were submitted to the Ukrainian government as entries in a competition among frontline drone pilots, with points and prizes for high scorers—a literal first-person shooter in the increasing video gamification of war…. Units earn points for each Russian soldier they incapacitate or kill and each weapon, vehicle or piece of military equipment they destroy. Points may be redeemed in an online government marketplace for more drones, with which to target more Russian forces.

It's an interesting story, and I have no reason to doubt its factual accuracy. But it struck me as oddly lacking in historical context. I'm no military historian, but let me lay out my thinking; I'd love to hear what more knowledgeable readers have to say about it.

As I understand it, the regular use of film recording to confirm kills of enemy aircraft dates back at least to World War II. And, famously, there was something of a competition for confirmed kill counts; military members have always valued a reputation for success, just like everyone else has.

To the extent there's any novelty with the Ukrainian drone story, it's just that the Ukrainian military is extending that system for monitoring the killing of aircraft to killing individual soldiers. And I'm not sure just how novel that is, either: I have heard that drones are sometimes used to confirm sniper kills, though I'm not sure just how pervasive or systematized that is.

As to the rewards, if the tangible reward is indeed just more drones, that sounds like a pretty obvious approach: Give more resources to the most effective units. As I understand it, Soviet Army units who had especially distinguished themselves were labeled "Guards" units, and got better equipment. But I expect that this was common in other times and places as well.

Beyond that, the Soviet Union famously gave monetary rewards per aircraft kill (particularly when confirmed photographically) during World War II; even Communists could appreciate the value of financial incentives. Here's an item from Stalin's order of June 17, 1942 (see also "Aces of the Great Patriotic [War]," as well as this August 19, 1941 order):

To incentivize the combat work of fighter pilots, [I order that there be] established starting June 20 of this year a monetary reward in the following amounts:
— for every downed enemy bomber pay 2000 rubles;
— for every downed enemy transport plane pay 1500 rubles;
— for every downed enemy fighter plane pay 1000 rubles ….

The payment for destroyed enemy airplanes is to be made in the event of confirmation by ground forces, photographs, and reports of multiple crews.

A reward in extra battlefield equipment seems to be, if anything, less noteworthy.

So, again, the Post article is an interesting and informative read, and indeed the particular details of the system (drone recordings of kills of individual soldiers for purposes of allocating extra weaponry). But I expected it to tie things more to the history of military videorecording, and of military rewards for successful action, rather than just frame it as "the first of its kind anywhere." I'd love to hear what readers who know more than I do about such matters think about this.

The post "Ukraine Turns Real-Life Kills into Video Game Thrills for Drone Pilots" appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Forum on "Emerging Applications of the Congressional Review Act"
articles.content:
上周，我参加了一场由联邦党人协会举办的关于"国会审查法的新兴应用"的论坛，论坛邀请了Boydeng Gray, PLLC的迈克尔·布什巴赫尔和乔治城大学法学院艾伦·B·莫里森教授，由吉布森邓恩律师事务所的劳拉·斯坦利主持。

讨论的重点是使用国会审查法撤销环境保护署向加州发放的联邦机动车排放标准豁免权。政府问责局认为这些豁免不符合CRA下的规则定义。迈克尔·布什巴赫尔持不同意见，其理由在这篇评论文章中有所解释。国会也持不同意见，并利用CRA撤销了这些豁免。

在论坛期间，我们讨论了撤销豁免的决定、这一决定的法律和政治影响、加州就撤销豁免提起的诉讼，以及这（以及其他最近的行动）对将来使用CRA约束行政机关的意义。未来使用CRA作为约束行政机构手段的含义。视频如下。

"国会审查法的新兴应用"论坛一文最初发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
Last week, I participated in a Federalist Society forum on "Emerging Applications of the Congressional Review Act," featuring Michael Buschbacher of Boyden Gray, PLLC and Professor Alan B. Morrison of the George Washington University Law School, moderated by Laura Stanley of Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher LLP.

The discussion focused on the use of the Congressional Review Act to rescind the Environmental Protection Agency's grant of waivers of preemption to California for its greenhouse gas vehicle emission standards. The Government Accountability Office did not think that the waivers qualified as rules under the CRA. Michael Bushcbacher disagreed, for reasons explained in this op-ed.  Congress disagreed too, and used the CRA to rescind the waivers.

During the forum we discussed the decision to rescind the waivers, the legal and political implications of this decision, California's lawsuit challenging the rescission, and what this (and other recent actions) mean for the future use of the CRA as a means to constrain administrative agencies. Video below.

The post Forum on "Emerging Applications of the Congressional Review Act" appeared first on Reason.com.
